title: Hospitality, Diotrephes, and Demetrius
subtitle: The elder commends Gaius, rebukes Diotrephes, and endorses Demetrius

heb.paragraph: The elder to the beloved Gaius - the term of affection occurs four times in this short letter. Gaius is genuinely loved.

heb.gloss: to prosper, succeed, have a good journey
heb.paragraph: I pray that all may go well with you and that you may be in good health - a conventional greeting elevated by genuine pastoral concern.
You are faithful in what you are doing for the brothers, even though they are strangers - hospitality to itinerant missionaries is commended.

panels.ctx: The elder to the beloved Gaius, whom I love in truth. Beloved, I pray that all may go well with you and that you may be in good health, as it goes well with your soul. For I rejoiced greatly when the brothers came and testified to your truth, as indeed you are walking in the truth. I have no greater joy than to hear that my children are walking in the truth. Beloved, it is a faithful thing you do in all your efforts for these brothers, strangers as they are, who testified to your love before the church. You will do well to send them on their journey in a manner worthy of God. For they have gone out for the sake of the name, accepting nothing from the Gentiles. Therefore we ought to support people like these, that we may be fellow workers for the truth.

cross.note: Gaius, who is host to me and to the whole church, greets you.
I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth.
Whoever receives you receives me; whoever gives a cup of cold water...
Do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers.

notes.note: The elder to beloved Gaius. I pray all goes well with you and your health, as it goes well with your soul. I rejoice that you walk in truth.
You are faithful in serving the brothers, even strangers. Send them on worthily. They went out for the name, accepting nothing from Gentiles. We ought to support them.
Calvin notes the prayer for physical health alongside spiritual health. The elder cares for the whole person.
Hospitality to missionaries is partnership in mission. Supporting them makes us fellow workers for the truth.
As it goes well with your soul - the prayer assumes spiritual prosperity and asks that physical well-being match it.
For the sake of the name (hyper tou onomatos) - the Name is Jesus. Early Christians went out as his representatives.
Yarbrough emphasizes that hospitality to missionaries was essential for gospel advance. Gaius faithful service enabled their ministry.
Kruse notes that accepting nothing from the Gentiles maintained the integrity of the mission. Financial independence prevented accusations of greed.

heb.gloss: to love to be first, desire preeminence
heb.paragraph: Diotrephes, who likes to put himself first - the compound word describes ambition for supremacy. He loves being number one.

heb.gloss: to cast out, expel, drive out
heb.paragraph: He even puts them out of the church - Diotrephes excommunicates those who show hospitality. He usurps authority.
Demetrius has received a good testimony from everyone - universal commendation contrasts with Diotrephes condemnation.

panels.ctx: I have written something to the church, but Diotrephes, who likes to put himself first, does not acknowledge our authority. So if I come, I will bring up what he is doing, talking wicked nonsense against us. And not content with that, he refuses to welcome the brothers, and also stops those who want to and puts them out of the church. Beloved, do not imitate evil but imitate good. Whoever does good is from God; whoever does evil has not seen God. Demetrius has received a good testimony from everyone, and from the truth itself. We also add our testimony, and you know that our testimony is true.

cross.note: They love the place of honor at feasts and the best seats.
Whoever would be great among you must be your servant.
Men will arise, speaking twisted things, to draw away disciples.
Do not admit a charge against an elder except on the evidence of two or three witnesses.

notes.note: Diotrephes loves to be first and does not acknowledge our authority. He talks wicked nonsense, refuses to welcome brothers, and expels those who do.
Do not imitate evil but good. Whoever does good is from God. Demetrius has good testimony from everyone, from truth itself, and from us.
Calvin sees Diotrephes as a warning against ecclesiastical tyranny. Love of preeminence corrupts leadership.
The contrast between Diotrephes and Demetrius is stark: one seeks self-glory, the other receives universal commendation.
Philoproteuo (loves to be first) is a rare compound verb. Diotrephes craves position and recognition.
The truth itself testifies to Demetrius - his life aligns with the gospel. Character and message match.
Yarbrough notes that Diotrephes represents a recurring problem: leaders who prioritize personal power over gospel mission.
Kruse emphasizes that the elder plans to confront Diotrephes personally. Serious problems require direct address.

heb.paragraph: I have much to write to you but do not want to write with pen and ink - the preference for face-to-face conversation echoes 2 John 12.
Peace be to you - the benediction of shalom. Peace with God and peace in community.
Greet the friends by name - personal attention to individuals in the community. Each is known and valued.

panels.ctx: I had much to write to you, but I would rather not write with pen and ink. I hope to see you soon, and we will talk face to face. Peace be to you. The friends greet you. Greet the friends, each by name.

cross.note: I have much to write but prefer to come and talk face to face.
I have called you friends, for all that I have heard from my Father I have made known to you.
Greet every saint in Christ Jesus.

notes.note: I have much to write but prefer face-to-face conversation. Peace be to you. The friends greet you. Greet the friends by name.
Calvin notes the warmth of the closing. Friends is an unusual designation, emphasizing the relational nature of the community.
Greet the friends by name (kat onoma) - the elder knows individuals personally. The church is not anonymous.
Yarbrough notes that the closing mirrors 2 John. Both letters are brief, personal, and anticipate face-to-face fellowship.
Kruse emphasizes the personal touch: each friend is to be greeted by name. Individual attention reflects love.

themes.note: Mission dominates through the hospitality theme. Hospitality to missionaries advances the gospel. Justice appears in the contrast between Diotrephes and Demetrius.

rows.label: vv. 9-12
rows.text: Rebuke of Diotrephes; commendation of Demetrius

lit.note: 3 John is a personal letter addressing hospitality and leadership. Gaius is commended, Diotrephes is rebuked, Demetrius is endorsed.
